name: pathlib
documentation:
**Attention:** this backport module isn't maintained anymore. If you want to report issues or contribute patches, please consider the [pathlib2](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pathlib2/) project instead.

# Description

pathlib offers a set of classes to handle filesystem paths. It offers the
following advantages over using string objects:

- No more cumbersome use of os and os.path functions. Everything can be
  done easily through operators, attribute accesses, and method calls.
- Embodies the semantics of different path types. For example, comparing
  Windows paths ignores casing.
- Well-defined semantics, eliminating any warts or ambiguities (forward vs.
  backward slashes, etc.).

# Requirements

Python 3.2 or later is recommended, but pathlib is also usable with Python 2.7
and 2.6.

# Install

In Python 3.4, pathlib is now part of the standard library. For Python 3.3
and earlier, `easy_install pathlib` or `pip install pathlib` should do
the trick.

# Examples

Importing the module classes:

```
>>> from pathlib import *
```

Listing Python source files in a directory:

```
>>> list(p.glob('*.py'))
[PosixPath('test_pathlib.py'), PosixPath('setup.py'),
 PosixPath('pathlib.py')]
```

Navigating inside a directory tree:

```
>>> p = Path('/etc')
>>> q = p / 'init.d' / 'reboot'
>>> q
PosixPath('/etc/init.d/reboot')
>>> q.resolve()
PosixPath('/etc/rc.d/init.d/halt')
```

Querying path properties:

```
>>> q.exists()
True
>>> q.is_dir()
False
```

Opening a file:

```
>>> with q.open() as f: f.readline()
...
'#!/bin/bash\n'
```

# Documentation

The full documentation can be read at [Read the Docs](https://pathlib.readthedocs.org/).

# Contributing

Main development now takes place in the Python standard library: see
the [Python developer's guide](http://docs.python.org/devguide/), and
report issues on the [Python bug tracker](http://bugs.python.org/).

However, if you find an issue specific to prior versions of Python
(such as 2.7 or 3.2), you can post an issue on the
[BitBucket project page](https://bitbucket.org/pitrou/pathlib/).

# History

## Version 1.0.1

- Pull request #4: Python 2.6 compatibility by eevee.

## Version 1.0

This version brings `pathlib` up to date with the official Python 3.4
release, and also fixes a couple of 2.7-specific issues.

- Python issue #20765: Add missing documentation for PurePath.with_name()
  and PurePath.with_suffix().
- Fix test_mkdir_parents when the working directory has additional bits
  set (such as the setgid or sticky bits).
- Python issue #20111: pathlib.Path.with_suffix() now sanity checks the
  given suffix.
- Python issue #19918: Fix PurePath.relative_to() under Windows.
- Python issue #19921: When Path.mkdir() is called with parents=True, any
  missing parent is created with the default permissions, ignoring the mode
  argument (mimicking the POSIX "mkdir -p" command).
- Python issue #19887: Improve the Path.resolve() algorithm to support
  certain symlink chains.
- Make pathlib usable under Python 2.7 with unicode pathnames (only pure
  ASCII, though).
- Issue #21: fix TypeError under Python 2.7 when using new division.
- Add tox support for easier testing.

## Version 0.97

This version brings `pathlib` up to date with the final API specified
in {pep}`428`. The changes are too long to list here, it is recommended
to read the [documentation](https://pathlib.readthedocs.org/).

:::{warning}
The API in this version is partially incompatible with pathlib 0.8 and
earlier. Be sure to check your code for possible breakage!
:::

## Version 0.8

- Add PurePath.name and PurePath.anchor.
- Add Path.owner and Path.group.
- Add Path.replace().
- Add Path.as_uri().
- Issue #10: when creating a file with Path.open(), don't set the executable
  bit.
- Issue #11: fix comparisons with non-Path objects.

## Version 0.7

- Add '\*\*' (recursive) patterns to Path.glob().
- Fix openat() support after the API refactoring in Python 3.3 beta1.
- Add a *target_is_directory* argument to Path.symlink_to()

## Version 0.6

- Add Path.is_file() and Path.is_symlink()
- Add Path.glob() and Path.rglob()
- Add PurePath.match()

## Version 0.5

- Add Path.mkdir().
- Add Python 2.7 compatibility by Michele Lacchia.
- Make parent() raise ValueError when the level is greater than the path
  length.

functions.name: pathlib._cached
documentation: Globbing helpers

classes.name: pathlib._Flavour

methods.name: pathlib._Flavour.join_parsed_parts
documentation:
Join the two paths represented by the respective
(drive, root, parts) tuples.  Return a new (drive, root, parts) tuple.

A flavour implements a particular (platform-specific) set of path
semantics.

methods.name: pathlib._WindowsFlavour.splitroot
documentation: Interesting findings about extended paths:
- '\\?\c:\a', '//?/c:\a' and '//?/c:/a' are all supported
but '\\?\c:/a' is not
- extended paths are always absolute; "relative" extended paths will
fail.

classes.name: pathlib._Accessor
documentation:
An accessor implements a particular (system-specific or not) way of
accessing paths on the filesystem.

methods.name: pathlib._NormalAccessor.symlink
documentation: Under POSIX, os.symlink() takes two args

methods.name: pathlib._NormalAccessor.readlink
documentation: Helper for resolve()

classes.name: pathlib._Selector

methods.name: pathlib._Selector.select_from
documentation:
Iterate over all child paths of `parent_path` matched by this
selector.  This can contain parent_path itself.

A selector matches a specific glob pattern part against the children
of a given path.

classes.name: pathlib._PathParents
documentation:
This object provides sequence-like access to the logical ancestors
of a path.  Don't try to construct it yourself.
Public API

classes.name: pathlib.PurePath

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.__new__
documentation:
Construct a PurePath from one or several strings and or existing
PurePath objects.  The strings and path objects are combined so as
to yield a canonicalized path, which is incorporated into the
new PurePath object.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.__str__
documentation:
Return the string representation of the path, suitable for
passing to system calls.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.as_posix
documentation:
Return the string representation of the path with forward (/)
slashes.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.__bytes__
documentation:
Return the bytes representation of the path.  This is only
recommended to use under Unix.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.as_uri
documentation: Return the path as a 'file' URI.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.anchor
documentation: The concatenation of the drive and root, or ''.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.name
documentation: The final path component, if any.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.suffix
documentation: The final component's last suffix, if any.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.suffixes
documentation: A list of the final component's suffixes, if any.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.stem
documentation: The final path component, minus its last suffix.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.with_name
documentation: Return a new path with the file name changed.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.with_suffix
documentation: Return a new path with the file suffix changed (or added, if none).

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.relative_to
documentation:
Return the relative path to another path identified by the passed
arguments.  If the operation is not possible (because this is not
a subpath of the other path), raise ValueError.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.parts
documentation:
An object providing sequence-like access to the
components in the filesystem path.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.joinpath
documentation:
Combine this path with one or several arguments, and return a
new path representing either a subpath (if all arguments are relative
paths) or a totally different path (if one of the arguments is
anchored).

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.parent
documentation: The logical parent of the path.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.parents
documentation: A sequence of this path's logical parents.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.is_absolute
documentation:
True if the path is absolute (has both a root and, if applicable,
a drive).

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.is_reserved
documentation:
Return True if the path contains one of the special names reserved
by the system, if any.

methods.name: pathlib.PurePath.match
documentation: Return True if this path matches the given pattern.

PurePath represents a filesystem path and offers operations which
don't imply any actual filesystem I/O.  Depending on your system,
instantiating a PurePath will return either a PurePosixPath or a
PureWindowsPath object.  You can also instantiate either of these classes
directly, regardless of your system.

classes.name: pathlib.Path

methods.name: pathlib.Path._raw_open
documentation:
Open the file pointed by this path and return a file descriptor,
as os.open() does.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.cwd
documentation:
Return a new path pointing to the current working directory
(as returned by os.getcwd()).
Public API

methods.name: pathlib.Path.iterdir
documentation:
Iterate over the files in this directory.  Does not yield any
result for the special paths '.' and '..'.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.glob
documentation:
Iterate over this subtree and yield all existing files (of any
kind, including directories) matching the given pattern.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.rglob
documentation:
Recursively yield all existing files (of any kind, including
directories) matching the given pattern, anywhere in this subtree.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.absolute
documentation:
Return an absolute version of this path.  This function works
even if the path doesn't point to anything.

No normalization is done, i.e. all '.' and '..' will be kept along.
Use resolve() to get the canonical path to a file.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.resolve
documentation:
Make the path absolute, resolving all symlinks on the way and also
normalizing it (for example turning slashes into backslashes under
Windows).

methods.name: pathlib.Path.stat
documentation:
Return the result of the stat() system call on this path, like
os.stat() does.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.owner
documentation: Return the login name of the file owner.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.group
documentation: Return the group name of the file gid.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.open
documentation:
Open the file pointed by this path and return a file object, as
the built-in open() function does.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.touch
documentation: Create this file with the given access mode, if it doesn't exist.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.chmod
documentation: Change the permissions of the path, like os.chmod().

methods.name: pathlib.Path.lchmod
documentation:
Like chmod(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
permissions are changed, rather than its target's.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.unlink
documentation:
Remove this file or link.
If the path is a directory, use rmdir() instead.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.rmdir
documentation: Remove this directory.  The directory must be empty.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.lstat
documentation:
Like stat(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
status information is returned, rather than its target's.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.rename
documentation: Rename this path to the given path.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.replace
documentation:
Rename this path to the given path, clobbering the existing
destination if it exists.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.symlink_to
documentation:
Make this path a symlink pointing to the given path.
Note the order of arguments (self, target) is the reverse of os.symlink's.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.exists
documentation: Whether this path exists.
Convenience functions for querying the stat results

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_dir
documentation: Whether this path is a directory.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_file
documentation:
Whether this path is a regular file (also True for symlinks pointing
to regular files).

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_symlink
documentation: Whether this path is a symbolic link.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_block_device
documentation: Whether this path is a block device.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_char_device
documentation: Whether this path is a character device.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_fifo
documentation: Whether this path is a FIFO.

methods.name: pathlib.Path.is_socket
documentation: Whether this path is a socket.
Filesystem-accessing classes
